concepts.title: Effects of heating
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Heating

1. When a gas is evolved

(a) Coloured gas:

(i) $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, (Brown) turns starch Iodine paper blue.

Substance: Nitrites and Nitrates of heavy metals

$\text{ e}\text{.g}\text{. }2\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CuO}+4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(ii) $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$(Reddish Brown) turns starch paper yellow

Substance bromide: $2\text{CdB}{{\text{r}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to 2\text{CdO}+2\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(iii) ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$(Violet) turns starch paper blue

Iodides: $2\text{Cd}{{\text{I}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to 2\text{CdO}+2{{\text{I}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(iv) $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ (Greenish yellow) bleaches moist litmus paper

Chlorides $\text{CuC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\underset{\left( moisture \right)}{\mathop{+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to }}\,\text{CuO}+2\text{HCl}$

$\downarrow \to \text{Cu}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+1/2\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

(b) Colourless gas (odourless)

(i) ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$-supports glowing

Alkali nitrates: $2\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\to 2\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(ii) $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}-$ Turns lime water milky

Carbonates/Oxalates $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\to \text{CaO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(c) Colourless gas (with odour)

(i) $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-$ turns red litmus blue

Ammonium salts: ${{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{HS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\uparrow $

(ii) $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}-$ Smell of burning sulphur, turns acidified ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ paper green

Sulphites, thiosulphates

$\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\to \text{CaO}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(iii) $\text{HCl}$ - pungent smell, white fumes with ammonia

hydrates chlorides

$\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot 6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to \text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2\text{HCl}\uparrow $

(iv) ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ - Smell of rotten eggs, turns lead acetate paper black

sulphides.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~\text{S}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to 2\text{NaOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}\uparrow $

2. A residue (oxide) is left and colour

(i) Yellow (on hot) & white (on cold) - $\text{ZnO}$

(ii) Reddish brown (hot) yellow (cold) $-\text{PbO}$

(iii) Black (hot) Red (cold) - $\text{HgO},\text{P}{{\text{b}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

(iv) Black (hot) Red Brown (cold)$-\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

3. Substance melts: Salts of alkali metals & salts having water of crystallisation.

4. Substance makes crackling noise: $\text{NaCl},\text{KI},\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}},\text{Ba}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$

5. Substance Swells: Alums, borates & phosphates

6. Substance sublimes & colour of sublimate is:

(a) White: $\text{HgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{H}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}},\text{A}{{\text{s}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}},\text{S}{{\text{b}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

(b) Yellow: $\text{A}{{\text{s}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{3}},\text{Hg}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$ turns red on burning with glass rod

(c) Blue Black or Violet: Iodides

concepts.title: Flame test
concepts.conceptBody:
Dry Tests

Dry tests are of great importance as these tests give clear indications of the presence of certain radicals. The following tests are performed in dry state:

(i) Flame test

(ii) Borax bead test

(iii) Microcosmic salt bead test

(i) Flame test

Some volatile salts impart characteristic colour to the non-luminous flame. The chlorides of the metals are more volatile in comparison to other salts. The metal chloride volatilises and its thermal ionisation takes place.

$\text{NaCl}\rightleftharpoons \text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\text{ }$

$\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

The cations impart a characteristic colour to the flame as these absorb energy from the flame and transmit the same as light of characteristic colour.

Procedure: The platinum wire fused in a glass rod is heated in the flame till it imparts colourless flame. This is achieved by dipping the wire in conc. $\text{HCl}$ and heating it. The process is repeated till it gives a colourless flame. The tip of the wire is now dipped in conc. $\text{HCl}$ and then into the substance. The tip of the wire is strongly heated in the non-luminous flame and the colour of the flame is observed by the naked eye.

Colour of flame Inference

1 Golden yellow Sodium

2 Violet Potassium

3 Brick red Calcium

4 Crimson red Strontium

5 Apple green Barium

6 Green with a blue centre Copper

Note: Flame test should not be performed in the presence of $\text{As},\text{Sb},\text{Bi},\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ as these radicals form alloy with platinum and hence, the wire is spoiled.

concepts.title: Borax Bead Test
concepts.conceptBody:
Borax bead test

On heating borax the colourless glassy bead formed consists of sodium metaborate and boric anhydride.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{B}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\cdot 10{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{\text{Heat}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{B}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{\text{Heat}}\underbrace{2\text{NaB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}_{\text{Glassy bead }}$

On heating with a coloured salt, the glassy bead forms a coloured metaborate in oxidising flame.

$\text{Cus}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CuO}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

$\text{CuO}+{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\left( Blue \right)}{\mathop{\underset{Copper\text{ }metaborate}{\mathop{\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{B}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}}}\,}}$

The metaborates posses different characteristic colours. The shade of the colour gives a clue regarding the presence of the radical.

However, in reducing flame the colours may be different due to different reactions. For example, copper metaborate may be reduced to colourless cuprous metaborate or to metallic copper, which appears red and opaque.

$2\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{B}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}+\text{C}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CuB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{CO}$

$2\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{B}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}+2\text{C}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Cu}+2~{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2\text{CO}$

Procedure: The free end of a platinum wire is coiled into a small loop and heated in the Bunsen flame until red hot. It is dipped in borax and again heated, when borax swells up and then fused into a glassy bead.

The bead is moistened with water and dipped in the coloured salt. It is now heated first in the oxidising flame and then in the reducing flame and colours are noted in both the flames in hot and cold conditions.

concepts.title: Microcosmic salt bead test
concepts.conceptBody:
Microcosmic salt bead test

This test is similar to borax bead test. When microcosmic salt is heated, a colourless transparent bead of sodium metaphosphate is formed.

$\text{Na}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)\text{HP}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\underset{\begin{smallmatrix} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{Sodium} \\ \text{metaphosphate} \end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{\text{NaP}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Sodium metaphosphate combines with metallic oxides to form orthophosphates which are usually coloured. The shade of the colour gives a clue regarding the presence of metal. Like borax bead test, colours are noted both in oxidising and reducing flames in hot and cold conditions.
